title: Coherent Nonreciprocal Valley Transport in Dirac/Weyl Semimetals
abstract: Nonreciprocal electronic transport, defined as a directional asymmetry between the forward and backward two-terminal responses, typically requires a built-in inversion-breaking feature of the host material or an applied field, such as magnetic order, magnetochiral coupling, polar lattice distortion, or a superconducting state. Here, we show that a single electrostatic barrier whose shape lacks inversion symmetry can drive coherent nonreciprocal transport in a Dirac or Weyl channel without any of these ingredients. The mechanism is geometric: across a barrier with two qualitatively distinct refraction interfaces (one vertical and one oblique), forward- and backward-propagating wave packets experience different Fermi-surface-mismatch sequences at the entrance and exit faces. Using coherent split-operator Dirac wave-packet simulations with realistic device parameters, we show that in a channel with isotropic (untilted) energy dispersion, an inversion-asymmetric (right-angle) triangular barrier produces strong charge-mode rectification, establishing its purely geometric origin. Adding a Dirac-cone tilt turns the same shape into a coherent valley-resolved diode whose dichroic structure flips sign across the Dirac point. Strikingly, a mirror-symmetric (isosceles) triangle with two oblique faces exhibits valley-polarized transmission while remaining exactly reciprocal. Oblique interfaces and tilt together do not suffice; the essential ingredient is a sequence of geometrically distinct interface types.
summary: This paper shows that nonreciprocal transport in Dirac and Weyl semimetals can be achieved purely through the geometry of electrostatic barriers rather than external magnetic fields. By using an asymmetric sequence of interfaces, one can create a device that acts as both a charge rectifier and a valley-polarized diode. This provides a gate-tunable, all-electrical method for controlling valley currents.
main_problem: Investigating how to achieve nonreciprocal electronic transport and valley-resolved transport in Dirac/Weyl semimetals without relying on magnetic fields, magnetic order, or broken time-reversal symmetry.
main_result: The study demonstrates that a purely geometric mechanism—using an inversion-asymmetric electrostatic barrier (a right-angle wedge) that sequences distinct interface types—can drive charge-mode rectification and, when combined with Dirac-cone tilt, create a valley-resolved diode.
method: The authors use a second-order split-operator Fourier propagation (Strang split-operator algorithm) to simulate the time-dependent dynamics of 2D Dirac wave-packets.
model_or_system: A 2D tilted Dirac/Weyl semimetal channel with mass-confinement walls and absorbing leads, featuring a gate-defined electrostatic barrier with varying geometries (rectangular, isosceles, and right-angle).
key_observables: Cumulative transmission (T) and reflection (R), charge-mode rectification (difference in forward/backward transmission), valley polarization (eta), and valley-resolved transmission.
important_parameters: Barrier height (V0), Fermi energy (EF), Dirac-cone tilt parameter (zeta), interface angle (alpha), and wave-packet collimation (sigma).
main_assumptions: The model assumes valley-diagonal potentials (neglecting intervalley scattering) and preserves time-reversal symmetry between the K and K' valleys.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows wave-packet probability density snapshots for forward vs. backward propagation; Figure 3 shows transmission and rectification plots for an untilted channel; Supplementary figures show the effect of barrier geometry and wave-packet collimation on the rectification ratio.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonreciprocity, defines the Hamiltonian and geometric models, presents numerical simulation methods, analyzes the geometric origin of rectification in untilted systems, extends the findings to valley-resolved transport in tilted systems, and concludes with a symmetry-based decomposition of the mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Device concept and direction-dependent interface composition. a, A right-angle barrier top-gate (gray) defines an inversion-asymmetric electrostatic barrier on a 2D Dirac/Weyl channel of longitudinal extent Lx ≈3.3 µm and transverse width Ly ≈3.3 µm, bounded transversely by mass- confinement walls. The barrier has full base width W = 500 nm at the lower channel boundary; at the injection level y = 0 its width is W/2 = 250 nm, with the vertical (V ) face at x = −125 nm and the oblique (O) face crossing y = 0 at x = +125 nm. Coherent Gaussian wave-packets are launched along the y = 0 axis from x = ∓823 nm, so the lead-to-barrier distances at the injection level are equal, dL = dR =...

Fig. 2. Direct visualization of the coherent nonreciprocity. Time-resolved snapshots of the wave-packet probability density |Ψ|2 for forward and backward injection through the right-angle V–O barrier on a tilted Dirac channel (ζy = 0.35, EF = 55 meV, V0 = 47 meV). In each row, time evolves along the propagation direction so that the spatial trajectory of the packet reads naturally. a, Forward propagation (V →O, high transmission). At t = t1 a Gaussian packet is launched from x = −823 nm on the y = 0 axis with central momentum along +ˆx (left subpanel). At t = t2 a compact, coherent transmitted lobe emerges on the right side of the barrier; the reflected component is weakly dispersed and...

Fig. 3. Charge-mode nonreciprocity in the absence of tilt. Coherent transport through the right-angle barrier on a Dirac channel with isotropic energy dispersion (wy = 0), plotted against the gate-defined barrier height V0. a, Normalized transmission T/(T + R) for forward injection (1→2) and backward injection (2→1); the shaded gap is ∆T = T →−T ←. The dotted vertical line marks EF . b, Charge-mode rectification ∆T(V0). For the parameters of these simulations [EF = 55 meV, σ = 132 nm, channel Lx ≈Ly ≈3.3 µm, barrier base W = 500 nm with vertical face at x = −125 nm and oblique-face apex at the bottom; injection points at x = ∓823 nm so that dL = dR = 698 nm at y = 0; see configs in the...

Fig. 4. Valley-resolved nonreciprocity in the tilted right-angle barrier. a, Coherent transmission for the four direction×valley channels, T → K , T → K′, T ← K , T ← K′, vs. barrier height V0. The two forward channels are nearly valley-blind, while the two backward channels develop separate notches on opposite sides of EF . b, Per-valley directional rectification ∆Tτ ≡T → τ −T ← τ . The K-rectification peaks below EF , the K′-rectification peaks above EF . c, Per-direction valley contrast TK −TK′. Forward injection is approximately valley-blind across the whole applied potential range; the backward direction acquires a sign-changing valley-dichroic structure with a node near EF . d,...

Fig. 5. Symmetry analysis: three barrier shapes on the same tilted channel. All three columns use the same channel, the same wave-packet, the same Fermi energy (EF = 55 meV), and the same tilt (ζy = 0.35); only the barrier shape changes. The three barriers are placed inversion-symmetrically about x = 0 in the injection plane, with dL = dR = 698 nm to the nearest barrier face at y = 0 and the same width-at-injection (W/2 = 250 nm); they differ only in shape away from y = 0 (V–V: parallel vertical faces; O–O: mirror-symmetric oblique faces; V–O: vertical + oblique).
